title: End of module: The Binomial Distribution

questions.text: Which of these is **not** one of the four conditions required for a binomial model $X \sim B(n,p)$?
choices: Exactly two possible outcomes on each trial
The trials are independent of each other
The number of trials must be at least 30
questions.explanation: The four conditions are: a fixed number of trials $n$; two outcomes per trial; a constant probability of success $p$; and independence between trials. There is no requirement that $n \ge 30$ — binomial models are used for small $n$ (as in this module) just as validly as large $n$.

questions.text: A bag contains 5 red and 5 blue counters. Three counters are drawn at random **without replacement**, and $X$ is the number of red counters drawn. Which condition of the binomial model does this situation fail?
choices: Constant probability of success and independence
It doesn't fail any condition — $X \sim B(3, 0.5)$ is a valid model
questions.explanation: Without replacement, the probability of drawing red changes after each draw depending on what has already been removed (e.g. it is $\frac{5}{10}$ on the first draw but becomes $\frac{4}{9}$ or $\frac{5}{9}$ on the second). This breaks both the constant-probability condition and the independence condition, so $B(3,0.5)$ is not a valid model here.

questions.text: A fair six-sided die is rolled 8 times, and $X$ is the number of times a six is rolled. Which distribution correctly models $X$?
choices: This cannot be modelled as binomial
questions.explanation: There are $n=8$ independent rolls, each with two outcomes ("six" or "not six") and a constant probability $p = \tfrac{1}{6}$ of success on every roll, so all four conditions hold and $X \sim B(8, \tfrac{1}{6})$.

questions.text: A biased coin has $P(\text{heads}) = 0.3$ and is tossed $n=6$ times. Let $X \sim B(6, 0.3)$ be the number of heads. Find $P(X = 2)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places.
$X \sim B(5, 0.4)$. Find $P(X = 2)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places.
$X \sim B(8, 0.25)$. Find $P(X = 3)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places.
$X \sim B(6, 0.3)$. Find $P(X \le 2)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places. (You may use $P(X=0)=0.1176$, $P(X=1)=0.3025$ and $P(X=2)=0.3241$, each to 4 d.p.)
$X \sim B(6, 0.3)$. Using the complement rule, find $P(X \ge 4)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places. (You are given $P(X \le 3) = 0.9295$ to 4 d.p.)
A large batch of components has a 20% defect rate. A random sample of 10 components is tested, and $X$ is the number of defective components. Find $\operatorname{Var}(X)$.
questions.explanation: $X \sim B(10, 0.2)$ (the batch is large, so the defect rate stays effectively constant across the sample). $\operatorname{Var}(X) = np(1-p) = 10 \times 0.2 \times 0.8 = 1.6$.
